meta.title: 5.4 Regression Detection Across Releases
meta.description: Learn how stratified eval sets — by framework, task type, complexity, and integration — surface category-level regressions that overall pass rates hide, and why explicit go/no-go gates with absolute minimums are the only defense against silent quality degradation across releases.

blocks.prompt:
You ship a new version of the agent that improves React code generation by 15%. But Vue code generation silently degraded by 8%. How do you catch this before users notice?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module teaches you to design stratified eval sets that surface category-level regressions that overall pass rates hide, and to set explicit go/no-go thresholds at the cell level. By the end, you'll be able to explain why an improving aggregate score can still be a release-blocking result — and specify the canary and A/B testing patterns that catch what offline evals miss.

An overall pass rate improvement can mask a release-blocking regression — which is why stratified eval sets, reporting independently per framework, task type, and complexity, are the only defense against improvements in one area quietly destroying another. For an AI code generation platform, this is particularly challenging because the model is a black box — you cannot inspect its internals to understand why a change in one capability affected another.

**The eval suite as a safety net**

The eval suite is only useful for regression detection if it is run consistently, covers all relevant dimensions, and has a principled pass/fail policy. A one-time eval run before a major release is not a safety net — it is a spot check.

A true safety net requires:

1. The full eval suite runs before every agent update that ships to users (not just major releases)
2. Results are automatically compared against the previous agent version
3. Any category that regresses beyond a defined threshold blocks the release
4. Results are stored historically so trends are visible over time

**Stratified eval sets**

A monolithic eval suite that scores "91% overall" hides category-level regressions. If React evals are 98% passing and Vue evals are 60% passing, the overall number looks acceptable while Vue users experience systematic failures.

Stratification means organizing eval prompts into distinct categories and reporting scores independently for each:

- **By framework**: React, Vue, Svelte, plain HTML/CSS
- **By task type**: Create new app, add a feature, fix a bug, modify styling
- **By complexity**: Simple (single component), medium (multi-page app), complex (full-stack with auth and database)
- **By integration**: Supabase, third-party APIs, custom backends

Each cell in this matrix gets its own pass rate. Regression detection operates at the cell level: if React × new-feature × medium complexity regresses from 90% to 75%, the eval system catches it even if overall React scores improved.

**Go/no-go gates**

Define explicit release criteria before any regression analysis:

- **Absolute minimum**: No cell can fall below 70% pass rate (if any cell is below 70%, the release is blocked regardless of direction of change)
- **Regression threshold**: No cell can regress by more than 3% from the previous version
- **Overall improvement**: The overall pass rate must be stable or improving (no net regression)

These thresholds should be set conservatively and adjusted over time based on operational experience. The cost of a false positive (blocking a release that was actually fine) is much lower than the cost of a false negative (shipping a regression that affects users).

## How to Think About This

The reason to stratify is not bureaucratic thoroughness — it's because the model's capability space is not uniform. An agent change that improves React generation may do so by deprioritizing patterns the Vue code path depends on. You cannot reason about this analytically; you can only observe it empirically. Stratified thresholds at the cell level make the observation automatic: the aggregate can improve while a specific cell falls through the floor, and the gate catches it before any user sees it.

---

blocks.question: A new agent version improves overall eval pass rate from 85% to 91%. The team wants to ship immediately. Before approving, what stratification breakdown would you require, and what specific pattern would make you block the release despite the overall improvement?
blocks.answer: Require the stratified breakdown by framework × task type × complexity. A blocking pattern would be any cell below the 70% absolute floor, or any cell that regressed more than 3% from the previous version — even if overall scores improved. Concretely: if React + create new app improved from 88% to 96% but Vue + modify styling dropped from 79% to 64%, the overall improvement is masking a Vue regression that violates both the absolute floor (below 70%) and the regression threshold (15-point drop). The correct response is to block the release and investigate the Vue regression before shipping.

blocks.content:
**Canary releases**

Even with a comprehensive eval suite, some regressions are only visible in production with real users and real prompts. Canary releases mitigate this by exposing a small percentage of users to the new agent before full rollout:

1. Ship the new agent to 5% of users (randomly selected, stratified across user segments)
2. Monitor real-world success metrics for 24-48 hours (session completion rate, error rate, user-triggered retries, explicit negative feedback)
3. Compare canary metrics against the control group (95% on the old agent)
4. If metrics are stable or improving, proceed to full rollout; if metrics degrade, roll back

Canary releases catch the failure modes that the eval suite misses: real user prompts that were not in the eval set, real infrastructure conditions that differ from eval environments, and user behavior changes that affect the agent's success rate.

**A/B testing agents**

For larger changes — new model versions, significant changes to the agent's prompting strategy — A/B testing provides statistically rigorous measurement:

1. Define the primary metric (session success rate, defined as: user does not retry, does not abandon, does not file feedback)
2. Randomly assign users to old agent (control) and new agent (treatment) for 1-2 weeks
3. Compute the metric for each group with confidence intervals
4. Ship the new agent only if the treatment group shows a statistically significant improvement or no regression

A/B testing is slower and more expensive than eval suite testing but provides ground truth about real user impact that no offline eval can replicate.

**The eval maintenance problem**

As Make's product evolves, eval prompts become stale. A prompt written 6 months ago ("build a React todo app") may be trivially easy for the current agent, providing no signal about capability edges. New product features (Supabase integration, custom domains) are not covered by old prompts.

The eval suite has a roadmap, just like the product. Someone must own:

- Retiring prompts that no longer challenge the agent
- Adding prompts that cover new product capabilities
- Updating reference outputs when correct behavior changes (e.g., when the design system updates)
- Balancing the eval set so it covers the full distribution of user prompts, not just the prompts that were easy to write

This is a significant ongoing investment. The Platform PM should advocate for eval suite maintenance as a quarterly planned activity, not reactive work.

---

blocks.question: The new agent version passes the go/no-go gates: no category regressed by more than 3%, overall pass rate improved from 87% to 89%. However, the canary rollout shows that session completion rate dropped by 2% in the canary group. How do you interpret this discrepancy, and what do you do?
blocks.answer:
*The discrepancy suggests the eval suite is missing failure modes that appear with real users. The 2% drop in session completion in the canary is a real signal from real users — it outweighs the eval suite result. Do not proceed to full rollout. Roll back the canary, investigate the specific sessions where users failed (what prompts were they using? where in the session did they abandon?), and create new eval cases from those failure patterns. The eval suite has a gap that must be filled before this agent version ships.*

---

blocks.title:
Look up how Vercel uses canary deployments for their own platform releases. Find a published post-mortem from any AI company (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) where a model update caused an unexpected regression. What was the regression? How was it detected? What eval improvements were made afterward?

---
blocks.solution: For Vercel canary deployments: you should find they use a staged rollout with monitoring gates at each step, similar to the module's canary pattern. For AI post-mortems: OpenAI and Anthropic have published incident reports on cases where model updates caused behavioral changes users didn't expect. Common patterns: a prompt that worked reliably in version N stops working in version N+1, detected first by users rather than internal evals. The typical response is adding the failure pattern as a regression test and tightening the behavioral gate. Key observation: in most published post-mortems, the regression was caught by user signal (complaints, support tickets) before eval signal — which is exactly the detection gap that canary monitoring and behavioral fingerprinting are designed to close. If you couldn't find a specific post-mortem, note the general pattern: most AI model regressions are soft quality issues, not hard failures, which means they rarely appear in system-level monitoring.

blocks.prompt:
An engineering lead says: "We run the eval suite on every PR, but it takes 4 hours. Developers are frustrated waiting. Can we just run a subset?" How do you decide which subset to run on each PR vs. reserving the full suite for release candidates?

---

questions: Recall a release you were part of where a regression reached users. In hindsight, what stratification or canary signal would have caught it before it shipped? What was the organizational reason it wasn't in place?

Think of a time you made a case for stricter quality gates — or a time someone made that case to you. What was the argument that actually moved the decision, and what would you use differently next time?

---

blocks.content:
Regression detection requires stratified eval sets (segmented by framework, task type, and complexity) with explicit go/no-go thresholds per cell, combined with canary releases to catch real-world failure modes that offline evals miss.

---

blocks.prompt:
Automated tests cover functional and visual quality, but roughly 40% of quality dimensions — code readability, aesthetic quality, UX judgment — resist automation. Human review of thousands of eval cases is impractical. The solution is LLM-as-judge: using a frontier model to evaluate another model's output. Next.

---

reviewQuestions.question: What does a stratified eval set organize eval prompts by, according to the module?
options: User account tier, geographic region, and device type
Framework (React, Vue, Svelte), task type (create, fix, modify), and complexity (simple, medium, complex)
Model version, token count, and session length
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module defines stratification as organizing eval prompts into distinct categories: by framework (React, Vue, Svelte, plain HTML/CSS), by task type (create new app, add a feature, fix a bug, modify styling), by complexity (simple, medium, complex), and by integration type. Each cell in this matrix gets its own pass rate.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice work! Stratification by framework, task type, and complexity is what makes category-level regression detection possible.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Stratification is about product dimensions — framework, task type, and complexity — not infrastructure or user account attributes. The goal is to ensure a regression in one framework (like Vue) can't hide behind strong performance in another (like React).

reviewQuestions.question: According to the module, what is the absolute minimum pass rate threshold below which any cell blocks a release?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module specifies that no cell can fall below a 70% pass rate — if any cell is below 70%, the release is blocked regardless of whether that cell is improving or declining. This is a floor threshold, separate from the 3% regression threshold that applies to direction of change.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it — 70% is the absolute floor. Any cell below it blocks the release, full stop.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The module sets the absolute minimum at 70%. If any stratification cell drops below that threshold, the release is blocked regardless of overall pass rate trends. This floor is separate from the regression threshold (no cell can regress by more than 3%).

reviewQuestions.question: Which pattern best describes why the module says the eval suite has 'a roadmap, just like the product'?
options: Eval suites must be rebuilt completely each time a new model version ships
Eval prompts grow stale as the agent improves and new product capabilities go uncovered, requiring planned maintenance to retire, add, and balance prompts
The eval roadmap determines which features can be built — features without eval coverage cannot ship
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module warns that a prompt written six months ago may be trivially easy for the current agent, and new features like Supabase integration or custom domains won't be covered by old prompts. Without active ownership, the eval suite drifts. The Platform PM should advocate for eval maintenance as a quarterly planned activity.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly right. An unmaintained eval suite gradually stops measuring what matters — it needs the same care as the product itself.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The eval suite follows the product's evolution — not the other way around. New capabilities need new prompts, old prompts that no longer challenge the agent need retiring, and the set must stay representative of real user prompts. That ongoing work is what earns it the name 'roadmap.'
